does: This paper investigates the internal mechanisms that large language models use to solve propositional logical reasoning tasks, specifically analyzing how Mistral and Gemma models (up to 27B parameters) handle problems requiring multi-fact combination. Using causal mediation analysis, the authors identify sparse circuits within these models that compute logical conclusions and decompose these circuits into interpretable sub-components. They provide fine-grained analysis of attention head functions across different layers, revealing whether reasoning is localized to specific network regions and whether models execute reasoning as sequential modular steps progressing through depth.
connects: This work extends mechanistic interpretability research that seeks to understand neural network behavior through circuit analysis, building on prior efforts to trace information flow and identify causally important components in transformer models. It responds to ongoing questions about how LLMs perform abstract reasoning despite their opacity, connecting to literature on multi-hop reasoning, compositional problem-solving, and the localization of capabilities within neural networks. The causal mediation methodology draws from established interpretability techniques adapted to probe logical reasoning specifically, adding to the growing body of work that uses minimal, well-defined tasks to reverse-engineer model computations.
enables: This mechanistic understanding of logical reasoning in LLMs opens pathways for improving model trustworthiness by making reasoning processes more transparent and auditable. The identified circuit decompositions could support better error analysis and targeted model improvements by pinpointing which sub-circuits fail on particular reasoning patterns. The methodology provides a template for analyzing other forms of reasoning and could lead to techniques for steering or controlling reasoning behavior at the component level. Understanding these sparse circuits may also inform more efficient architectures or training procedures that explicitly encourage modular, interpretable reasoning pathways.
